name: Loop of Tarbes
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Tarbes are situated at the foothills of the Pyrenees, offering a diverse landscape for cyclists. The region features challenging mountain passes, serene greenways, and quiet roads. Terrain varies from well-paved surfaces in valleys to demanding ascents with significant elevation gains in the higher areas. This area is characterized by its proximity to iconic Pyrenean climbs and picturesque river valleys. Best road cycling routes around Tarbes The most popular road cycling route is Loop of Tarbes, a 41.2 miles (66.3 km) trail that takes 3 hours 25 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain, characteristic of the Pyrenean foothills. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Small loop of lake Gabas, an easy 21.9 miles (35.3 km) path. This route offers a gentler ride on mostly well-paved surfaces around a scenic lake. Local road cyclists also love the Voie verte des Gaves – Hautacam loop from Ossun, a 47.6 miles (76.6 km) trail leading through a mix of greenway and challenging mountain ascent, often completed in about 4 hours 18 minutes. Road cycling around Tarbes is defined by Pyrenean mountain passes, tranquil greenways, and scenic river valleys. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy rides to challenging climbs. The routes in Tarbes are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 350 reviews. More than 13,000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Tarbes's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Tarbes?
A: There are over 880 road cycling routes around Tarbes, offering a wide range of options for all skill levels. These routes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 350 reviews.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes near Tarbes?
A: The region around Tarbes offers diverse terrain. You'll find challenging mountain passes like the iconic Col du Tourmalet and Col d'Aspin, known for their significant elevation gains and steeper gradients compared to the Alps. There are also serene greenways and well-paved surfaces in the valleys, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e854892881/small-loop-of-lake-gabas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Small loop of lake Gabas&lt;/a&gt;, providing gentler rides.

Q: Are there easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Tarbes?
A: Yes, Tarbes offers several easier routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e854892881/small-loop-of-lake-gabas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Small loop of lake Gabas&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 21.9-mile (35.3 km) path on mostly well-paved surfaces. The Voie Verte des Gaves, connecting Lourdes to Pierrefitte-Nestalas, also provides a gentler, scenic ride through the Pyrenees. Within Tarbes itself, dedicated cycle paths and initiatives like the "Vélorue" make it easy and safe to explore the city by bike.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: For experienced riders seeking a challenge, the region offers legendary Tour de France climbs. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39955158" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Col du Tourmalet – Tourmalet Pass loop from Tarbes&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40983963" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Voie verte des Gaves – Hautacam loop from Ossun&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40948890" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Climb to Col d'Aspin – Col d'Aspin loop from Tarbes&lt;/a&gt; feature significant elevation gains and demanding gradients.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes around Tarbes?
A: Many routes around Tarbes are designed as loops. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e850088207/loop-of-tarbes" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Loop of Tarbes&lt;/a&gt; is a 41.2-mile (66.3 km) difficult route. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e854892881/small-loop-of-lake-gabas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Small loop of lake Gabas&lt;/a&gt; offers an easier, scenic option. You can also find challenging loops like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39955158" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Col du Tourmalet – Tourmalet Pass loop from Tarbes&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What natural features and landmarks can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: Road cycling routes around Tarbes offer access to stunning natural beauty and landmarks. You can cycle past picturesque lakes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5606547" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arrêt-Darré Lake&lt;/a&gt;, Lac de Soues, and Lac de Bours, which provide scenic views of the Pyrenean massifs. The routes also lead through river valleys and offer glimpses of the Pyrenees National Park, home to sites like the Cirque of Gavarnie. Charming towns like Bagnères-de-Bigorre also provide cultural interest.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Tarbes?
A: The region is generally excellent for road cycling, especially from spring through autumn. The Pyrenean climbs are best enjoyed when free of snow, typically from late spring to early autumn. Summer offers warm weather, but spring and fall can provide pleasant temperatures and fewer crowds. Always check local weather conditions, especially for mountain passes, as they can change rapidly.

Q: Are there routes that offer panoramic viewpoints?
A: Yes, many routes in the Tarbes area provide spectacular panoramic views, especially those ascending into the Pyrenees. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40982761" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Pic du Midi – Tourmalet Pass loop from Ossun&lt;/a&gt; offers breathtaking vistas. The Col du Tourmalet and Col d'Aspin are renowned for their majestic scenery. You can also find scenic viewpoints like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4533003" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Panorama of Miramont&lt;/a&gt; along some routes.

Q: Is it easy to find parking or access routes by public transport in Tarbes?
A: Tarbes is well-connected, making it accessible for cyclists. The city has dedicated cycle paths and is situated near a motorway network. For specific routes, parking availability can vary, but many starting points for popular cycling routes are typically accessible by car. For more detailed information on getting around Tarbes by bike, you can consult the local tourism office: &lt;a href="https://www.tarbes-tourisme.fr/en/plan-my-trip/getting-around-tarbes/by-bike/" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;tarbes-tourisme.fr&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Tarbes?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.3 stars. Reviewers often praise the diverse landscape, from challenging mountain passes to serene greenways, and the quiet roads, especially on many climbs where traffic is not a significant concern. The blend of iconic Pyrenean climbs and picturesque river valleys makes it a favorite destination.

Q: Are there any routes that pass by lakes or rivers?
A: Absolutely. Tarbes is situated on the Adour River, and its banks offer pleasant routes like the CaminAdour. Many cycling routes also pass by beautiful lakes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e854892881/small-loop-of-lake-gabas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Small loop of lake Gabas&lt;/a&gt; is a prime example, offering a scenic ride around a tranquil lake. Other lakes like Lac de Soues and Lac de Bours are also easily accessible and provide picturesque views.

Q: Can I find cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the routes?
A: While specific cafes are not listed for every route, the region around Tarbes is dotted with charming towns and villages. Thermal resorts like Bagnères-de-Bigorre and Cauterets offer various amenities, including cafes and restaurants, making them ideal stopping points on longer rides. Exploring these towns will likely lead you to refreshment options.
tips: This little road with little traffic, offering at the top a magnificent view of the Pyrenees, is a gentle and interesting prologue before going to climb the passes of the region.
